name: efficientdet

members.extended:
EfficientDet (Tan et al., CVPR 2020) applies compound scaling to object
detection.  Each compound coefficient φ jointly scales the backbone
(EfficientNet-Bφ), the BiFPN width/depth, and the prediction head
depth/resolution.

parameters.name: num_classes
parameters.description:
Foreground classes (background not counted separately
            — EfficientDet uses sigmoid + focal loss).

notes:
Image → EfficientNet backbone (P3–P7 feature maps)
  → BiFPN (D_bifpn repeats, W_bifpn channels, fast normalised fusion)
BiFPN outputs (P3–P7) → Class prediction head (shared conv)
                       → Box prediction head  (shared conv)
weight_i / (ε + Σ weight_j) — learnable positive weights (ε=1e-4)

members.name: efficientdet_config
members.summary: Build the canonical EfficientDet-D{phi} config from the compound table.
members.extended:
EfficientDet's compound scaling (Tan et al., 2020) maps a single
scalar $\phi \in \{0, \ldots, 7\}$ to the BiFPN channel
count, BiFPN repeat count, head repeat count, and backbone output
channel counts in lock-step.  This helper looks the row up and
materialises an `EfficientDetConfig`.

parameters.name: phi
parameters.description:
Compound-scaling coefficient.  Values `0` through `7`
select EfficientDet-D0 (smallest, default) through
EfficientDet-D7 (largest) per the paper's Table 1.

returns.description: Frozen config ready to feed into the model factory.

notes:
.. [1] Tan, Pang & Le, *EfficientDet: Scalable and Efficient Object
   Detection*, CVPR 2020.

members.name: EfficientDetForObjectDetection

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, targets: list[dict[str, Tensor]] | None = None)

returns.description:
`ObjectDetectionOutput`:
`logits`    : (B, A, K) per-class sigmoid logits (A = total anchors).
`pred_boxes`: (B, A, 4) decoded xyxy boxes.
`loss`      : focal + smooth-L1 when targets provided.

methods.name: postprocess
methods.summary: Per-class NMS on raw sigmoid predictions.
methods.extended: Returns list of per-image result dicts with "boxes", "scores", "labels".

members.summary: EfficientDet object detector (Tan et al., CVPR 2020).
members.extended:
A family of compound-scaled single-stage detectors combining an
EfficientNet backbone, a **BiFPN** (bidirectional weighted feature
pyramid) neck, and shared classification + box regression heads
applied to 5 feature levels (P3-P7).  The family is parameterised
by a compound coefficient $\varphi \in \{0, 1, \dots, 7\}$
that simultaneously scales backbone depth / width, BiFPN width and
repeat count, head depth, and input resolution — yielding D0-D7
variants that span ~4M to ~52M parameters and trade speed for
accuracy along a Pareto-optimal curve.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use the per-$\varphi$ factories
(`efficientdet_d0` through `efficientdet_d7`) for the
paper-cited compound-scaled variants.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.efficientdet import efficientdet_d0
>>> model = efficientdet_d0()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 512, 512)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape[-1], out.pred_boxes.shape[-1]
(90, 4)
notes:
See Tan et al., "EfficientDet: Scalable and Efficient Object
Detection", CVPR 2020 ([arXiv:1911.09070](https://arxiv.org/abs/1911.09070)).  The BiFPN's defining
feature is weighted feature fusion at each node:

$$
O = \sum_{i} \frac{w_i}{\epsilon + \sum_j w_j} \cdot I_i,
$$
with non-negative weights $w_i$ learned end-to-end (the paper
calls this "fast normalized fusion").  Compound scaling follows

$$
\begin{aligned}
    W_\mathrm{BiFPN} &= 64 \cdot 1.35^\varphi, &
    D_\mathrm{BiFPN} &= 3 + \varphi, \\
    D_\mathrm{head}  &= 3 + \lfloor \varphi / 3 \rfloor, &
    R_\mathrm{input} &= 512 + 128\varphi,
\end{aligned}
$$
so D0 ($\varphi = 0$) trains on 512x512 inputs while D7
($\varphi = 7$) uses 1536x1536.  Training uses focal loss
$\mathrm{FL}(p_t) = -\alpha_t (1 - p_t)^\gamma \log p_t$ on
the K-channel class output plus smooth-$L_1$ on box deltas.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: backbone
attributes.description:
EfficientNet trunk producing C3 / C4 / C5 features at strides
8 / 16 / 32.

attributes.name: p3_proj, p4_proj, p5_proj
attributes.description:
1x1 conv + BatchNorm channel projections from backbone widths
to `config.fpn_channels`.

attributes.name: p6_pool, p7_pool
attributes.description: 2x downsamples producing P6 and P7 at strides 64 and 128.

attributes.name: bifpn
attributes.description:
`config.fpn_repeats` `_BiFPNLayer` blocks performing
weighted bidirectional top-down + bottom-up feature fusion.

attributes.name: cls_head, box_head
attributes.description:
Shared per-level prediction heads with `config.head_repeats`
3x3 separable convolutions; outputs `K * num_anchors` and
`4 * num_anchors` channels respectively.

attributes.name: _anchor_gen
attributes.description:
Anchor generator covering 5 levels with
$|\mathrm{scales}| \times |\mathrm{ratios}|$ anchors per cell.

members.name: efficientdet_d0
members.extended:
Builds the smallest compound-scaled EfficientDet variant: EfficientNet-B0
backbone, BiFPN with 64 channels and 3 repeats, head depth 3, and a
512x512 input resolution.  Approximately 3.9M parameters; COCO test-dev
mAP of 33.8% (paper Table 2) at 2.5 BFLOPs.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently ignored.

returns.description:
Detector with the D0 configuration applied (or with `overrides`
merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.efficientdet import efficientdet_d0
>>> model = efficientdet_d0()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 512, 512)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.pred_boxes.shape[-1]
4
notes:
See Tan et al., "EfficientDet: Scalable and Efficient Object Detection",
CVPR 2020 ([arXiv:1911.09070](https://arxiv.org/abs/1911.09070)).  D0 is the smallest of the eight
compound-scaled variants and the recommended baseline for
mobile / edge deployment.

members.name: efficientdet_d1
members.extended:
Compound-scaled variant: EfficientNet-B1 backbone, BiFPN 88 channels
with 4 repeats, head depth 3, 640x640 input.  Approximately 6.6M
parameters; COCO test-dev mAP of 39.6% (paper Table 2).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently ignored.

returns.description: Detector with the D1 configuration applied.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.efficientdet import efficientdet_d1
>>> model = efficientdet_d1()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 640, 640)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.pred_boxes.shape[-1]
4

members.name: efficientdet_d2
members.extended:

with 5 repeats, head depth 3, 768x768 input.  Approximately 8.1M
parameters; COCO test-dev mAP of 43.0% (paper Table 2).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently ignored.

returns.description: Detector with the D2 configuration applied.

examples:

>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 768, 768)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.pred_boxes.shape[-1]
4

with 6 repeats, head depth 4, 896x896 input.  Approximately 12.0M

Compound-scaled variant: EfficientNet-B6 backbone, BiFPN 384 channels

members.name: efficientdet_d7
members.extended:
The largest compound-scaled variant: EfficientNet-B6 backbone (shared
with D6), BiFPN 384 channels with 8 repeats, head depth 5, and a
1536x1536 input.  Approximately 51.9M parameters; COCO test-dev mAP
of 53.7% (paper Table 2) — the headline accuracy result.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently ignored.

returns.description: Detector with the D7 configuration applied.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.efficientdet import efficientdet_d7
>>> model = efficientdet_d7()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 1536, 1536)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.pred_boxes.shape[-1]
4

summary: EfficientDet — scalable compound-scaled detector (Tan et al., CVPR 2020).
extended: Paper: "EfficientDet: Scalable and Efficient Object Detection"
citation: Tan, Mingxing, et al. "EfficientDet: Scalable and Efficient Object Detection." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020, pp. 10781–10790.
theory:
EfficientDet jointly applies the **compound scaling** idea of
EfficientNet to the entire detection pipeline — backbone, feature
network, and prediction heads — instead of scaling any single
axis (width, depth, resolution) in isolation.  A single compound
coefficient $\varphi$ controls all three:



$$
D_{\mathrm{bifpn}} = 3 + \varphi, \quad
W_{\mathrm{bifpn}} = 64 \cdot 1.35^{\varphi}, \quad
D_{\mathrm{head}} = 3 + \lfloor \varphi / 3 \rfloor, \quad
R_{\mathrm{input}} = 512 + 128 \varphi,
$$

so EfficientDet-D0 through D7 form a family with predictable
accuracy–latency trade-offs without manual hyper-tuning.

The feature network is a **BiFPN** (Bi-directional Feature
Pyramid Network).  Unlike a one-way FPN, BiFPN adds bottom-up
paths back into the pyramid and learns **fast normalised
fusion** weights so each merged level is a convex combination
of its inputs:



$$
O = \sum_{i} \frac{w_i}{\varepsilon + \sum_j w_j} \cdot I_i,
\qquad w_i \ge 0,
$$

where the ReLU on $w_i$ (here written as a non-negativity
constraint) keeps fusion stable without an explicit softmax,
and the small $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$ prevents division
by zero.  BiFPN is then **stacked** $D_{\mathrm{bifpn}}$
times.

The detection heads (classification + box) share weights across
levels and use sigmoid + **focal loss** for classification, the
same anchor-based formulation as RetinaNet.  Combined with the
EfficientNet-B\ $_{\varphi}$ backbone, the result is the
Pareto-optimal one-stage detector family of its time —
EfficientDet-D7 reaches 55.1 AP on COCO at far fewer FLOPs than
contemporaries.
